front: STAR prompt: 'Tell me about a time you led a technical initiative without formal authority.'
back: Situation: scope the system, team size, stakes. Task: your specific ownership (not the team's). Action: 3-5 concrete steps emphasizing decisions and tradeoffs, not activities. Result: quantified (latency, cost, users, deploy frequency). End with one coordination detail (who you aligned with) and one thing that didn't go well + what you learned. Senior signal: you owned the outcome, not the ticket.
senior_nuance: Juniors say 'we did X'. Seniors say 'I decided X because Y, even though Z was tempting'. Always include a tradeoff you rejected — it proves you had options and picked one deliberately.
quote_to_say: I had no direct reports on this, but I was accountable for the number.

front: STAR prompt: 'Tell me about a conflict with a coworker and how you resolved it.'
back: Pick a real technical disagreement, not a personality clash. Situation: the decision at stake + why both sides had merit. Task: your role in resolving it. Action: (1) sought to understand their reasoning in a 1:1, (2) identified the shared goal, (3) proposed a data-driven tiebreaker (prototype, metric, spike), (4) committed to the outcome either way. Result: the decision made + the working relationship after. Never frame the other person as the villain.
senior_nuance: The best conflict story ends with 'they were partially right and I updated my view'. Interviewers screen out candidates who always win conflicts — that signals inability to be wrong.
quote_to_say: We disagreed, so we agreed on what evidence would settle it.

front: STAR prompt: 'Tell me about your biggest professional failure.'
back: Pick a real failure with business impact, not a fake 'I cared too much'. Situation: what you shipped / decided. Task: your ownership. Action: what you did that turned out wrong + why it seemed right at the time. Result: concrete impact (outage duration, revenue, team time, user trust). Then the learning: what process, guardrail, or instinct you changed. Name the change, don't just say 'I learned'.
senior_nuance: Interviewers test whether you can distinguish 'bad outcome from reasonable decision' (acceptable) from 'bad outcome from bad decision' (must own it). Say which one yours was. Refusing to name a real failure is itself a red flag.
quote_to_say: I shipped it, it broke, and here's the guardrail I added so it can't happen again.

front: STAR prompt: 'Tell me about a time you worked on an ambiguous problem with no clear spec.'
back: Situation: vague ask (e.g., 'make it faster', 'reduce cost', 'users are complaining'). Task: turn it into something measurable. Action: (1) interviewed 2-3 stakeholders to find the real problem, (2) picked one leading metric, (3) wrote a one-page problem framing and got sign-off, (4) shipped smallest useful thing in 2 weeks, (5) iterated on signal. Result: metric moved + clarified scope for next team. Senior signal: you converted ambiguity into a crisp contract.
senior_nuance: Ambiguity is not 'I figured it out alone in the dark'. It's 'I built the map'. Show that you created shared understanding, not that you were a lone hero.
quote_to_say: The ask was fuzzy, so step one was agreeing on what 'done' looked like.

front: STAR prompt: 'Tell me about a time you disagreed with your manager.'
back: Pick a disagreement where you were partially right AND you respected the decision process. Situation: the call your manager was about to make. Task: raise the concern without undermining. Action: (1) asked for 1:1 time, (2) laid out risk + alternative + cost of each, (3) asked what data would change their mind, (4) disagreed and committed when they decided. Result: either (a) they updated, or (b) they didn't and you supported the call — and what the outcome taught you.
senior_nuance: The phrase 'disagree and commit' is table stakes at senior. Also OK: 'I escalated one level up with my manager's knowledge' — shows you know when a decision warrants more eyes. Never tell a story where you went behind your manager's back.
quote_to_say: I disagreed, I said so in writing, and then I committed to the plan.

front: STAR prompt: 'Tell me about a time you scaled a system.'
back: Situation: the load curve (e.g., '10x traffic in 6 months'), the bottleneck (DB writes, CPU, network, lock contention). Task: your specific slice. Action: (1) measured before touching anything (p50/p99, flamegraph, slow-query log), (2) formed a hypothesis, (3) shipped the cheapest fix first (cache, index, batch, connection pool), (4) re-measured, (5) only then considered rewrites. Result: latency / throughput / cost numbers before vs after. Senior signal: you measured first and rewrote last.
senior_nuance: Never open with 'we rewrote it in Rust'. That signals you reach for the big hammer. The senior move is 'here's the 20-line change that got us 80% of the win'.
quote_to_say: Before I changed anything, I wanted the numbers to tell me where to look.

front: STAR prompt: 'Tell me about a time you had to fight for tech debt work against feature pressure.'
back: Situation: the debt (what it cost the team — on-call pages, velocity, bugs), the competing feature ask. Task: make the tradeoff visible and get a decision. Action: (1) quantified the debt in business terms (hours lost per sprint, incident MTTR, churn), (2) proposed a concrete time-boxed slice (not 'a full rewrite'), (3) tied it to an upcoming feature that would be cheaper after, (4) got PM/EM buy-in in writing. Result: the fix shipped + downstream velocity change.
senior_nuance: 'Fighting for tech debt' at senior means translating it into the language the business speaks. Never pitch 'the code is ugly'. Pitch 'this is costing us N engineer-days per quarter and blocking feature X'.
quote_to_say: I stopped calling it tech debt and started calling it a recurring tax.

front: STAR prompt: 'Tell me about a time you had to hit a very tight deadline.'
back: Situation: the deadline (contract date, launch, conference), the gap between scope and time. Task: protect the must-haves. Action: (1) cut scope aggressively by naming P0/P1/P2 with the PM, (2) de-risked the riskiest piece first (not the easiest), (3) communicated a confidence percentage weekly, not a 'we're on track', (4) pre-wrote the rollback plan. Result: shipped on time with the P0 set + what was deferred and why. If you missed, own it and say what you changed.
senior_nuance: Seniors distinguish 'deadline because arbitrary' from 'deadline because external contract'. For arbitrary ones, you negotiate scope. For real external ones, you protect quality on the critical path. Never say 'we worked weekends' — that's a planning failure, not a badge.
quote_to_say: I cut scope on day 3, not day 30.

front: STAR prompt: 'Tell me about a time you influenced a team you didn't own.'
back: Situation: a cross-team dependency or shared system where you needed change from another team. Task: get them to prioritize it. Action: (1) went to the other team with a written problem statement + proposed API, (2) framed the ask in their metrics, not yours, (3) offered to do part of the work in their codebase, (4) found the senior-most engineer on their side as an ally before escalating. Result: the change shipped + the ongoing relationship. Senior signal: you built trust, not a ticket.
senior_nuance: The senior version of cross-team is 'I did the first 20% of their work for them so accepting the rest was the easy choice'. Never say 'I escalated to their director' as the first move — that's a last resort, not a first resort.
quote_to_say: I wrote the first draft in their repo so saying yes was cheaper than saying no.

front: STAR prompt: 'Tell me about someone you mentored and their growth.'
back: Pick one specific engineer (not 'the team'). Situation: where they were, a specific gap (scoping, testing, code review, communication). Task: your role (formal mentor, senior on the team, tech lead). Action: (1) identified the gap via observation, not assumption, (2) agreed with them on a growth area, (3) created low-stakes reps (pair on a design doc, lead one review), (4) gave feedback weekly with specifics, (5) gradually handed over ownership. Result: a concrete level-up (promotion, owned system, ran an incident). Credit them, not yourself.
senior_nuance: The tell for real mentoring is specifics about the other person's growth, not yours. If your mentoring story is really about how clever you were, the interviewer will notice.
quote_to_say: My job was to make their next project feel one size too big — but only by one size.

front: STAR prompt: 'Tell me about a time you had to say no to a stakeholder.'
back: Situation: the ask (from a PM, exec, customer), why it felt urgent. Task: decide whether to commit. Action: (1) restated the ask to confirm understanding, (2) surfaced the real cost (what it would displace), (3) offered an alternative that met the underlying need at lower cost, (4) made the tradeoff the stakeholder's decision with data, not yours alone. Result: the outcome + preserved relationship. Senior 'no' is always paired with an alternative.
senior_nuance: 'No, because here's a better path' beats 'no, because we're busy'. Never say no to something you haven't understood — always restate the need before declining.
quote_to_say: I can't do X by Friday, but I can do Y by Thursday, which solves the same thing.

front: STAR prompt: 'Walk me through an incident you handled.'
back: Pick a real SEV1 or SEV2. Situation: what broke, blast radius, when you were paged. Task: incident commander vs investigator vs fixer — name your role. Action: (1) mitigated before diagnosing (rollback, feature flag, traffic shift), (2) established comms channel + external status, (3) ran parallel tracks: one person on root cause, one on customer comms, (4) called it after stable for N minutes. Result: MTTR, customer impact, postmortem outcomes. Name the permanent fix AND the process change.
senior_nuance: Interviewers listen for 'mitigate before you diagnose'. Juniors often try to find root cause while customers are still impacted. Also listen for blameless framing — 'the deploy system let this through' beats 'Alice pushed bad code'.
quote_to_say: Step one was stop the bleeding. Root cause could wait thirty minutes.
